name: Ronda - circular walk along the walls and viewpoints of the Tajo
name_original: Ronda – paseo circular por las murallas y miradores del Tajo
region_intro: Hiking around Ronda offers access to diverse natural features within the Serranía de Ronda mountains. The region is characterized by the dramatic El Tajo gorge, deep river valleys, and rugged limestone peaks. It is bordered by the Sierra de Grazalema Natural Park and the Sierra de las Nieves Natural Park, both UNESCO Biosphere Reserves. These areas feature Mediterranean forests, karstic formations, and rivers like the Guadiaro. Best hiking trails around Ronda The most popular hiking route is Ronda - circular walk along the walls and viewpoints of the Tajo, a 2.3 miles (3.8 km) trail that takes 1 hour 12 minutes to complete. This route provides views of the El Tajo gorge and the city's historic walls. Another top favourite among local hikers is Pico Torrecillas – circular via the Cañada de las Ánimas - Sierra de las Nieves National Park, a difficult 9.3 miles (14.9 km) path. This trail ascends to Pico Torrecilla, offering panoramic views within a pristine mountainous environment. Local hikers also love the Tajo de Ronda (Ronda Gorge) – Parador of Ronda loop from Ronda, a 4.3 miles (7.0 km) trail leading through the iconic El Tajo gorge, often completed in about 2 hours 2 minutes. Hiking around Ronda is defined by dramatic gorges, rugged limestone peaks, and Mediterranean forests. The network of trails offers options for various ability levels, from gentle walks to challenging mountain ascents. The routes in Ronda are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from more than 1300 reviews. More than 8200 hikers have used komoot to explore Ronda's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Ronda?
A: Ronda offers a wide selection of hiking opportunities, with over 100 routes available. These trails cater to various skill levels, from easy strolls to challenging mountain ascents, ensuring there's something for every hiker.

Q: What kind of terrain and natural features can I expect on hikes in Ronda?
A: Hiking in Ronda provides access to diverse landscapes within the Serranía de Ronda mountains. You'll encounter the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5150674" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Tajo gorge&lt;/a&gt;, deep river valleys, and rugged limestone peaks. The region is bordered by the Sierra de Grazalema Natural Park and the Sierra de las Nieves Natural Park, both UNESCO Biosphere Reserves, featuring Mediterranean forests, karstic formations, and rivers like the Guadiaro.

Q: Are there any easy or family-friendly hiking options in Ronda?
A: Yes, Ronda offers many accessible options. Approximately 41 of the trails are considered easy. For a gentle introduction, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995617740/ronda-circular-walk-along-the-walls-and-viewpoints-of-the-tajo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ronda - circular walk along the walls and viewpoints of the Tajo&lt;/a&gt;, which is a relatively short and scenic route providing views of the El Tajo gorge and the city's historic walls.

Q: What are the options for more challenging hikes in the Ronda area?
A: For experienced hikers seeking a challenge, Ronda and its surroundings offer difficult routes, including ascents within the Sierra de las Nieves National Park. A notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995685820/pico-torrecillas-circular-via-the-canada-de-las-animas-sierra-de-las-nieves-national-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Torrecillas – circular via the Cañada de las Ánimas&lt;/a&gt;, which ascends to Pico Torrecilla and offers panoramic views.

Q: Are there circular hiking routes around Ronda?
A: Yes, many trails in Ronda are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An excellent example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33793966" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajo de Ronda (Ronda Gorge) – Parador of Ronda loop from Ronda&lt;/a&gt;, which leads through the iconic El Tajo gorge.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Ronda?
A: The hiking routes in Ronda are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average score of 4.3 stars from over 1300 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the dramatic scenery, the well-maintained paths, and the unique blend of natural beauty with historical landmarks.

Q: What notable landmarks or attractions can I see while hiking near Ronda?
A: Many trails offer views of or pass by significant landmarks. You can see the iconic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/251032" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente Nuevo (New Bridge)&lt;/a&gt;, the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/207634" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arab Bridge of Ronda&lt;/a&gt;, and the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2970565" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuenca Gardens&lt;/a&gt;. Some routes also provide unique perspectives of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5150674" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajo de Ronda (Ronda Gorge)&lt;/a&gt; from below.

Q: Are there any hikes that offer views of the Puente Nuevo from different perspectives?
A: Absolutely. While many routes offer views of the Puente Nuevo, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33795529" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Puente Nuevo – Ronda City Walls loop from Ronda&lt;/a&gt; is specifically designed to showcase this architectural marvel from various angles, including perspectives from below the gorge.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Ronda?
A: The best time to hike in Ronda is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is mild and pleasant, ideal for exploring the trails. Spring brings wildflowers, while autumn offers comfortable temperatures and beautiful foliage. Summers can be very hot, and winters can be cooler and wetter, especially at higher elevations.

Q: Are there any hikes that explore the wider Serranía de Ronda region?
A: Yes, beyond the immediate vicinity of Ronda, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1356954349/arriate-and-the-ventilla-stream-circular-from-los-prados" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arriate and the Ventilla stream — circular from Los Prados&lt;/a&gt; take you into the broader Serranía de Ronda, allowing you to experience the diverse Mediterranean forests, olive groves, and traditional landscapes of the region.
tips: Plaza Duquesa de Parcent, believed to be the square on which the Roman Forum originally stood, is now the main square in Ronda's old town. It has a wealth of monuments, such as the Church of Santa Maria la Mayor, the Town Hall once a military prison and other churches and monasteries dotted around the square.

The Church of Santa Maria la Major de Ronda, raised to the category of collegiate by King Ferdinand the Catholic, stands on the old aljama mosque of the city, a building of the thirteenth century, of which remnants of the mihrab y are still preserved of the wall, with ataurique decoration. Formerly it could have been a Roman temple.
Completely restored, towering, a thorn in the enemy's side when it comes from the east.
The arch Arco de Felipe V or Felipe V is part of the city walls of Ronda, Spain. It is named after the King of Spain Philip V 1683 – 1746.

Located in the Historic Site of Ronda and close to the Padre Jesús neighborhood, this building was rebuilt at the end of the eighteenth century. It has a splendid Baroque façade made of stone masonry with a lintelled door, Corinthian columns and a large balcony made of Ronda wrought iron. The façade is topped with a broken pediment that houses nude figures of clear Indian influence.

The Casa del Rey Moro is within walking distance of the Palacio del Marques de Salvatierra. The House of the Moorish King dates back to the 18th century. However, you can only view the outside of the large building. The surrounding garden is also worth a visit.
The best viewpoint in all of Ronda The Mirador del Puente Nuevo is considered the best viewpoint in the entire Andalusian municipality of Ronda.
The road bridge spans the Río Guadalevín over the approximately 120 meter deep El Tajo gorge. The bridge separates the old town from the younger part of the city.

A most famous place where you can admire a gorge with steep rocks is in the middle of the city of Ronda. Here the Guadalevin River runs through the El Tajo Gorge. The gorge separates two parts of the city, Mercadillo on one side and La Ciudad on the other.
